coredata.dc:title: Study on the molecular interaction of graphene quantum dots with human serum albumin: Combined spectroscopic and electrochemical approaches
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shan
Qiu, Hangna
Lu, Shuangyan
Zhu, Fawei
Xiao, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attention in biological and biomedical applications due to their super properties, but their potential toxicity investigations are rarely involved. Since few studies have addressed whether GQDs could bind and alter the structure and function of human serum albumin (HSA), the molecular interaction between GQDs and HSA was systematically characterized by the combination of multispectroscopic and electrochemical approaches. GQDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA via static mode. The competitive binding fluorescence assay revealed that the binding site of GQDs was site I of HSA. Some thermodynamic parameters suggested that GQDs interacted with HSA mainly through van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions, and protonation might also participate in the process. As further revealed by FT-IR spectroscopy and circular dichroism technique, GQDs could cause the global and local conformational change of HSA, which illustrated the potential toxicity of GQDs that resulted in the structural damage of HSA. Electrochemical techniques demonstrated the complex formation between GQDs and HSA. Our results offered insights into the binding mechanism of GQDs with HSA and provided important information for possible toxicity risk of GQDs to human health.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Human serum albumin
Binding interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of HSA by GQDs
Binding interaction between GQDs and HSA
Conformational change of HSA induced by GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption, normalized excitation, and emission spectra of GQDs. Photographs of emission colors of GQDs under 365nm UV radiation.

Steady-state fluorescence spectra of 2.0μM HSA in the presence of different concentration of GQDs. The inset corresponds to the Stern–Volmer plot of HSA with GQDs at 298K. The concentrations of GQDs were (1–15): 0; 17.1; 34.2; 51.3; 68.4; 85.5; 171; 256; 342; 427; 513; 598; 684; 769; 855μM, respectively.
Stern–Volmer plots (A), modified Stern–Volmer plots (B), and van’t Hoff plot (C) of GQDs–HSA system.

UV–vis absorption spectra of HSA (A), GQDs (B), GQDs–HSA system (C), and the difference absorption spectra between GQDs–HSA system and GQDs (D). The concentrations of HSA and GQDs were 2.0μM and 85.5μM, respectively.
Variation of fluorescence anisotropy (r) of GQDs with increasing concentrations of HSA. The concentration of GQDs was 42.7μM.

Effect of site-specific probe to GQDs–HSA system, (A) warfarin; (B) ibuprofen. (C) The modified Stern–Volmer plots of GQDs–HSA system without and with warfarin or ibuprofen. The concentrations of warfarin, ibuprofen, and HSA were all 2.0μM. The concentrations of GQDs were (1–11): 0; 8.55; 17.1; 25.6; 34.2; 42.7; 51.3; 59.8; 68.4; 76.9; 85.5μM, respectively.
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of HSA and GQDs–HSA system. The concentrations of HSA and GQDs were 2.0 and 85.5μM, respectively.

(A) FT-IR spectra of HSA, GQDs, GQDs–HSA system and the summed absorption spectra between HSA and GQDs. The concentrations of HSA and GQDs were 1.0mM and 12.8mM, respectively. (B) CD spectra of HSA and GQDs–HSA system. The concentration of HSA was 2.0μM. The concentrations of GQDs were (a–d): 0; 8.55; 17.1; 34.2μM, respectively.

(A) CV of bare GCE and GQDs modified GCE in the presence of HSA. The concentrations of HSA were (1–9): 0; 10; 20; 30; 40; 50; 60; 70; 80nM, respectively. Insert was the linear relationship existed between 1/ΔI p and 1/c. (B) EIS of GQDs modified GCE with different concentrations of HSA. The concentrations of HSA were (1–9): 0; 10; 20; 30; 40; 50; 60; 70; 80nM, respectively. Insert was the linear relationship existed between c/R ct and c.
Stern–Volmer quenching constants K SV, associative binding constants K a and relative thermodynamic parameters.
Associative binding constants K a of GQDs–HSA system at 298K in the presence of some common ions. The concentrations of the adopted ions and HSA were all 2.0μM.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: K 0 and K are the apparent binding constants of GQDs–HSA system alone, and GQDs–HSA system in the present of common ion, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Three-dimensional fluorescence spectral characteristics of HSA and GQDs–HSA system.
Fractions of different secondary structures determined by SELCON3.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The subscripts “R” and “D” represent “ordered” and “disordered”, respectively.

head.ce:title: Study on the molecular interaction of graphene quantum dots with human serum albumin: Combined spectroscopic and electrochemical approaches

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Virology, College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Guangxi Teachers Education University, Nanning 530001, PR China. Tel.: +86 771 3908065; fax: +86 771 3908065.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted great attention in biological and biomedical applications due to their super properties, but their potential toxicity investigations are rarely involved. Since few studies have addressed whether GQDs could bind and alter the structure and function of human serum albumin (HSA), the molecular interaction between GQDs and HSA was systematically characterized by the combination of multispectroscopic and electrochemical approaches. GQDs could quench the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA via static mode. The competitive binding fluorescence assay revealed that the binding site of GQDs was site I of HSA. Some thermodynamic parameters suggested that GQDs interacted with HSA mainly through van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions, and protonation might also participate in the process. As further revealed by FT-IR spectroscopy and circular dichroism technique, GQDs could cause the global and local conformational change of HSA, which illustrated the potential toxicity of GQDs that resulted in the structural damage of HSA. Electrochemical techniques demonstrated the complex formation between GQDs and HSA. Our results offered insights into the binding mechanism of GQDs with HSA and provided important information for possible toxicity risk of GQDs to human health.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel kind of graphene nanomaterial, fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted tremendous attention in diverse research fields during the recent years [1–3]. Similar to other graphene nanosheets, GQDs exhibit some excellent properties such as high electrical/thermal conductivity, high surface area, and convenience for surface grafting by using the π–π conjugated network or abundant surface functional groups [4,5]. Compared with some convenience semiconductor QDs, GQDs further show some unique properties, for example, excellent solubility, high photostability, and good biocompatibility. These properties make GQDs to be widely used as excellent fluorescent probes in biological and biomedical fields, also as effective drug carriers in cancer therapy [6–13].

Due to the tremendous focus on the applications of GQDs in disease diagnosis and biomedical imaging, there has been increasing interest in estimating the potential toxicity risk of GQDs in biological systems. Although it has been reported in a number of previous literatures that graphene or graphene oxide may induce severe cytotoxicity and lung diseases [14–16], the toxicity related investigations or results regarding biological effects of GQDs are rarely reported. Recently, Nurunnabi et al. [17] studied the in vivo biodistribution and potential toxicity of GQDs for the first time, and their results revealed that carboxylated GQDs did not cause appreciable toxicity to the treated animals. However, to the best of our knowledge, the toxicity research of GQDs at the molecular level has not been reported to date. A thorough quantitative and qualitative investigation of the interaction between GQDs and protein is quite necessary since this information will determine the targeting efficiency, distribution, and clearance of GQDs for diagnostics that serves as the basis in determining their in vivo toxicity.

Human serum albumin (HSA), which is the most important and the major carrier protein of the circulatory system, has been widely used as model protein for many biophysical and biochemical researches [18,19]. Since, HSA contributes significantly to colloid osmotic blood pressure and participates in the transportation, distribution, and metabolism of drugs and nanoparticles [20], the affinities of these exogenous ligands toward HSA can determine the transportation, distribution, and metabolism of them to a certain extent. Consequently, investigation on the affinity and the interaction mechanism of these exogenous ligands with HSA may provide some useful information for both therapeutic effective of drugs in pharmacology and biological effects of nanoparticles to organisms. For these reasons, the interactions of different drugs and nanoparticles with HSA have been investigated largely in recent years [21–25]. However, as far as we know, the interaction of GQDs with HSA has not been reported until now.

In this article, the unprecedented study of molecular interaction between GQDs and HSA under physiological condition was reported. In order to quantitatively and qualitatively determine the affinity of GQDs to HSA, and to investigate the thermodynamics property of their interaction, detailed investigation of HSA–GQDs association was carried out by using spectroscopic and electrochemical approaches. The stoichiometry of HSA and GQDs binding was also illuminated. Particularly, the conformational changes of HSA induced by GQDs were studied by using fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy and circular dichroism (CD) technique, which may provide structural information that elucidate the biological effects of GQDs. This research may provide valuable information to the growing concerns regarding the potential toxicity risk of GQDs on human health.

1.0mgmL−1 GQDs were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China) and used as received. The molecular weight of GQDs was expressed by the molecular weight of benzene, around 78gmol−1. HSA, warfarin, and ibuprofen were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Sigma, St. Louis, MO, USA). HSA was dissolved in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) and then kept in refrigerator at 4°C. The concentration of HSA was determined from UV–vis absorption spectrum by using the extinction coefficient at 280nm of 36600Lmol−1 cm−1. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm was produced by passing through a Milli-Q Integral 5 unit followed by a Millipore-Q Academic purification set (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the whole experiments.

Steady-state fluorescence analyses were performed on PerkinElmer LS 55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA) equipped with 20KW Xenon discharge lamp as light source and thermostatic bath (DKB 501S, Shanghai Jing Hong Laboratory Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 300–450nm upon excitation at 295nm when HSA samples were titrated with GQDs. Titrations were performed manually by using trace syringes. The widths of the excitation and the emission slits were set to 10.0nm and 3.5nm, and each spectrum was the average of three scans. 1.0cm quartz cells were used for all measurements. Also, respective blanks of PBS were used for the correction of all fluorescence spectra. The experiments were repeated and found to be reproducible within experimental errors.

The binding site investigation experiments were performed by using different site-specific probes through keeping the concentrations of both HSA and site-specific probe constant but varying the concentration of GQDs alone. The widths of the excitation and the emission slits were set to 10.0nm and 3.5nm for GQDs–HSA-warfarin system, and the widths of the excitation and the emission slits were set to 10.0nm and 5.0nm for GQDs–HSA-ibuprofen system. The steady-state fluorescence spectra of GQDs–HSA-probe system were recorded and the corresponding association constants of GQDs–HSA-probe system were evaluated, which could decide the binding site of GQDs.

Fluorescence anisotropy measurements were performed at room temperature. Each titration point of the sample equilibration at least twenty times with an integration time of 10min was collected. The fluorescence anisotropy of the interaction between GQDs and HSA was followed at λ ex 350nm and λ em 447nm.

Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of HSA and GQDs–HSA system were performed with the emission wavelength range was 200–500nm and the excitation wavelength range was 200–350nm with increment of 5nm. The widths of the excitation and the emission slits were set to 10.0nm and 4.5nm.

UV–vis absorption spectra of HSA, GQDs, and GQDs–HSA system were recorded on Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Inc., Australia) equipped with 1.0cm quartz cells. The recorded wavelength was ranging from 450 to 200nm. The solutions of the blank buffer and sample were placed in the reference and sample cuvettes, respectively. The difference absorption spectrum between GQDs–HSA system and GQDs was obtained through recording the UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs–HSA system by using GQDs as the reference.

FT-IR spectra of HSA and GQDs–HSA system were recorded on Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo, USA) equipped with a zinc selenide attenuated total reflection (ATR) accessory, a deuterated triglycine sulfate detector, and a KBr beam splitter. All spectra were recorded in the range of 1300–1800cm−1 with 128 interferograms to ensure a good signal-to-noise ratio. All spectra were taken via ATR method with a resolution of 4cm−1. The subtraction of the reference spectrum from the spectrum of PBS buffer was carried out in accord with the criteria that a straight baseline was obtained between 2000 and 1750cm−1 [26].

CD spectra were recorded on Jasco J-810 automatic recording CD spectropolarimeter (Jasco, Tokyo, Japan) equipped with 1.0cm quartz cells. The CD profiles were obtained employing a scan speed of 500nmmin−1 and response time of 0.5s. Each spectrum was the average of three successive scans and was corrected by PBS. Appropriate baseline corrections in CD spectra were made.

Cyclic voltammograms (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were performed on CHI-660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Inc., Shanghai, China) with conventional three-electrode electrochemical testing system. The working electrode was GQDs modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), whereas Ag/AgCl electrode served as the reference electrode and platinum wire was used as the counter electrode. The electrolyte solution was 5.0mMK3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 and 10mM KCl. In addition, 0.05% Tween 20 (volume ratio) was present in the electrolyte solution for the elimination of the nonspecific adsorption of HSA on GQDs modified GCE (Figs. S1 and S2).

For GQDs modified GCE preparation, 5μL GQDs solution (1.0mgmL−1) was dropped onto the surface of GCE. After 4h incubation at room temperature, the electrode was washed by ultrapure water and then the GQDs modified GCE was obtained. For CV and EIS measurement, different amounts of HSA solution were added continuously into the electrolyte solution with 0.05% Tween 20 and then stirred for 5min. The reaction system was at rest for 3min before testing. CV were recorded at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 and EIS were measured within the frequency range from 0.1 to 100KHz. All measurements were repeated three times with different GQDs modified GCE at 298K.

The representative UV–vis absorption, normalized excitation, and emission spectra of GQDs were shown in Fig. 1 . Due to the n → π* electronic transition of GQDs, a typical absorption band centered at 340nm possibly resulted from the trapping of excited state energy by the surface state led to strong emission [2]. It could be seen that, with an excitation wavelength of 350nm, the purchased GQDs exhibited an obvious, narrow, and almost symmetrical emission spectrum with a strong emission maximum at 447nm without tails on the right-hand side of emission spectra. The digital picture inserted in Fig. 1 further elucidated that GQDs were possessed of high water-solubility and strong blue fluorescence property. Furthermore, GQDs were nearly spherical with good size distribution and excellent monodispersity (Fig. S3). Since, the average sizes of GQDs were around 1.2nm, GQDs can be easily absorbed by cells and incorporated into biomedical regions, which might result in the potential toxicity of GQDs to organisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of HSA by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been demonstrated that the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA is mainly ascribed to the lone tryptophan (Trp) residue. The intrinsic fluorescence of HSA is very sensitive to its local microenvironment and can be easily affected by other molecules [27], so the steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy is used to obtain the information about the local microenvironment of HSA [28,29], and further confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism between HSA and GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 , HSA showed a strong and obvious fluorescence emission band around 350nm with the excitation wavelength of 295nm, but GQDs exhibited almost no fluorescence under the same conditions. Meanwhile, the intrinsic fluorescence intensity of HSA was quenched regularly when GQDs were added into the solution, and the quenching effect was mainly dependent on the concentration of GQDs. The concentration-dependent fluorescence change indicated the potentially greater toxicity to HSA at higher concentrations of GQDs.

Inset of Fig. 2 showed that at low concentrations (from 0 to 85.5μM), the results agreed with the Stern–Volmer equation excellently. However, at higher concentrations, the results depart from the initial linearity. In order to avoid the inner filter effects, the experiment was carried out within the linear part of Stern–Volmer dependence and the concentration of GQDs varied from 0 to 85.5μM.

Since, the reported fluorescence quenching mechanism usually includes collisional process (dynamic quenching) and/or ground state complex formation (static quenching) between quencher and fluorophore that can be easily distinguished by the difference on temperature dependence [30,31], the fluorescence quenching of HSA by GQDs at three different temperatures can be analyzed through the Stern–Volmer equation [32]:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
Herein, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of HSA in the absence and presence of GQDs, K SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of GQDs, respectively. Hence, the K SV value can be calculated by the linear regression of a plot of F 0/F against [Q]. The plots of Stern–Volmer equation at three different temperatures were shown in Fig. 3 A and the corresponding K SV values were listed in Table 1 . It could be seen that the Stern–Volmer quenching constant decreased accordingly with the temperature rising, demonstrating the moderate influence of temperatures on the fluorescence quenching and the probable static quenching mode of HSA by GQDs.

For static quenching process, the quenching data can be further analyzed through the modified Stern–Volmer equation [33]:
(2)
F
0
F
0
− F
=
F
0
Δ F
=
1
f
a
K
a
1
[ Q ]
+
1
f
a
Herein, f a represents the mole fraction of solvent-accessible fluorophore. K a, which represents the effective quenching constant for the accessible fluorophore [34], is analogous to the associative binding constant for the quencher-acceptor system [35,36]. As shown in Fig. 3B, the dependence of F 0/ΔF on the reciprocal value of the quencher concentration [Q]−1 was linear with the slope equaling the value of (f a K a)−1 and the ordinate equaling the value of f a −1. The corresponding associative binding constants K a were calculated from the quotient of the ordinate f a −1 and the slope (f a K a)−1 through Eq. (2). As shown in Table 1, the associative binding constant also decreased with the increasing temperature, which was in accordance with the variation trend of K SV. Furthermore, the linear correlation coefficient of modified Stern–Volmer regression curves was higher than that of Stern–Volmer regression curves, demonstrating that Eq. (2) dedicated to static quenching mode was more suitable to the description of HSA and GQDs interactions.Moreover, the associative binding constant K a increased in the present of common ions (Table 2 ), implying the stronger binding between GQDs and HSA under the synergistic affect from ions. These results suggested that GQDs would have a longer storage time in blood plasma and would be difficultly cleared from the blood, which enhanced the toxicity of GQDs in clinical therapy to some extent.

In addition, the fluorescence quenching mechanism can be verified by careful examination of UV–vis absorption spectrum of fluorophore. Dynamic quenching merely affect the excited states of fluorophore and no variations occur in the absorption spectrum of fluorophore, but static quenching often result in the perturbation of the absorption spectrum of fluorophore [28,37]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the UV–vis absorption spectrum of HSA (curve A in Fig. 4) in the range of 230–350nm and the difference absorption spectrum between GQDs–HSA system and GQDs (curve D in Fig. 4) at the same wavelength range could not be superposed perfectly within the experimental error, reconfirming the static quenching mechanism and the ground state complex formation between HSA and GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding interaction between GQDs and HSA
ce:section.ce:para:
The binding forces between exogenous ligands and HSA usually include hydrophobic, van der Waals interactions, hydrogen bonds, and electrostatic interactions [38], which can be elucidated by the thermodynamic parameters. If the enthalpy change (ΔH) remains constant during the studied temperature range, both the enthalpy change (ΔH) and the entropy change (ΔS) can be calculated from the van’t Hoff equation:
(3)
ln ⁡
⁡
K
a
= −
Δ H
RT
+
Δ S
R
The free energy change (ΔG) can be calculated from the following equation:
(4)
Δ G = Δ H − T Δ S

In Eq. (3), K a is analogous to the associative binding constant at the corresponding temperature T, and R is the gas constant, respectively. It could be seen from Fig. 3C that a good linear relationship between ln K a and 1/T was existed. The calculated ΔH, ΔS, and ΔG values were all summarized in Table 1. Due to the negative values of ΔG, the binding process between GQDs and HSA was occurred spontaneously. In addition, both the negative values of ΔH and ΔS indicated that van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bond interactions played major roles during this binding reaction [39,40]. Moreover, protonation might participate in the conjugation of GQDs with HSA.

Moreover, fluorescence anisotropy measurements can give details about binding phenomenon [41]. The fluorescence anisotropy (r) is defined by:
(5)
r =
(
I
VV
− G
I
VH
)
(
I
VV
+2 G
I
VH
)
(6)
G =
I
VH
I
VV
where G is the instrument grating correction factor, I VV and I VH are the intensities obtained with the excitation polarizer oriented vertically and the emission polarizer oriented vertically and horizontally, respectively. As indicated in Fig. 5 that a marked increment in fluorescence anisotropy value with increasing concentration of HSA, which reflected that the rotational diffusion of GQDs was restricted significantly in the microenvironments of HSA and the Brownian motion of GQDs was decreased at the present of HSA [42]. The high fluorescence anisotropy value (r =0.25) suggested that GQDs was binding at a motional restricted site on HSA.

As reported previously, HSA contains two binding sites for exogenous ligands known as Sudlow’s sites I and II, which are located in subdomain IIA and IIIA, respectively [18,20]. The precise binding sites of GQDs on HSA can be determined from the competitive displacement experiments by using warfarin (site I) and ibuprofen (site II) as site-specific probes [21,43]. The results shown in Fig. 6 indicated that the emission peak of HSA red-shifted obviously and the fluorescence intensity of HSA increased significantly after the addition of warfarin, while both the emission peak and the fluorescence intensity of HSA remained almost constant when ibuprofen was present. Furthermore, the associative binding constant decreased from 2.46×104 Lmol−1 to 4.92×103 Lmol−1 and 2.38×104 Lmol−1 in the present of warfarin and ibuprofen, respectively. As evident from these results, GQDs could be significantly displaced by warfarin and the binding site of GQDs was mainly located within site I.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conformational change of HSA induced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The conformation of HSA affected by GQDs can be investigated through three-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy. According to Fig. 7 , fluorescence peak 1 (λ ex/em =290.0/346.3nm) and fluorescence peak 2 (λ ex/em =235.0/346.9nm) represented the fluorescence spectral behavior of Trp residue and the polypeptide backbone structures of HSA, respectively [29]. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensities of both peak 1 and peak 2 decreased (Table 3 ), while the fluorescence intensity of peak 2 decreased dramatically after the addition of GQDs, suggesting that the interaction of GQDs with HSA changed the polar environment of Trp residue partly but affected the polypeptide backbone structures of HSA drastically. The entire phenomenon and the analyzing data proved the binding of GQDs with HSA that resulted in the structural damage of HSA.

FT-IR spectroscopy is another effective method to directly analyze the variation of the secondary structure of HSA. It could be seen from Fig. 8 A that HSA exhibited two main amide bands around 1652cm−1 (CO stretch) and 1548cm−1 (CN stretch, NH bending mode) related with the secondary structure of proteins [24]. Meanwhile, GQDs exhibited two main absorption bands around 1568cm−1 (CO stretch) and 1390cm−1 (CH stretch) [44,45]. In comparing with the absorption spectrum of HSA and the summed absorption spectrum between HSA and GQDs, the absorption spectrum of GQDs–HSA system exhibited a tiny wavelength blue shift (from 1652 or 1651 to 1649cm−1). In addition, compared with the absorption spectrum of HSA, the absorption spectrum of GQDs–HSA system showed an obvious wavelength red shift (from 1548 to 1558cm−1). But compared with the summed absorption spectrum between HSA and GQDs, the absorption spectrum of GQDs–HSA system exhibited an apparent wavelength blue shift (from 1565 to 1558cm−1). These results reconfirmed that GQDs interacted with CO, CN, and NH groups in the polypeptides of HSA that resulted in the structure rearrangement of HSA [46].

CD spectroscopy is a quantitative technique to investigate the conformation of HSA. As shown in Fig. 8B, HSA exhibited two negative absorption bands around 208nm and 222nm which represented the typical α-helical structure of HSA, and resulted from the n → π* and π → π* electron transfer for the peptides bonds of the α-helix [24]. The addition of GQDs led to the decrease of the band intensities at all wavelengths of HSA, which indicated that the interaction of GQDs with HSA resulted in the conformational changes of HSA. Furthermore, CD results can be expressed in terms of mean residue ellipticity (MRE) using the following equation:
(7)
MRE =
Observed CD
( m deg ⁡ )
C
p
nl
× 10
Herein, C p represents the concentration of protein, n is the number of amino acid residues (585 for HSA) and l is the path length, respectively. In order to quantify the contents of different types of HSA, CD spectra were sequentially analyzed by algorithm SELCON3 [24]. As shown in Table 4 , the α-helices content decreased but other secondary structure contents increased with the increment of the concentration of GQDs, which indicated the reduction of the biological activity of HSA and the destruction of the hydrogen bonding networks of HSA upon the interaction with GQDs.

Cyclic voltammetry can be applied to the analyses of the interaction of HSA with GQDs [47]. As indicated in Fig. 9 A, both bare GCE and GQDs modified GCE exhibited good electrochemical response of K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 with well-defined reversible peaks, which indicated the successful surface modification of GQDs on GCE through π–π stacking conjugation [5]. Furthermore, when HSA was administrated into the K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 electrolyte solution with 10mMKCl and 0.05% Tween 20, the redox peak currents decreased significantly and the redox peak potentials shifted slightly, which resulted from the reduction of the diffusion coefficient of K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 after the binding between HSA and GQDs [48].Moreover, after the reaction with HSA, GQDs modified GCE was washed with ultrapure water and subsequently dipped into fresh K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 solution with 10mMKCl and 0.05% Tween 20 again, both the redox peak currents and the redox peak potentials varied greatly compared with the redox peak currents and the redox peak potentials of GQDs modified GCE alone (Figure S4). Furthermore, after the reaction with HSA, GQDs modified GCE exhibited almost constant redox peak currents and the redox peak potentials before or after washing with ultrapure water. These results suggested that the complex formation between GQDs and HSA took place at the surface of GCE, which agreed well with the results revealed from spectroscopic approaches.

According to values of the redox peak currents with different concentrations of HSA obtained from Fig. 9A, the relationship between the reciprocal of the current drop (ΔI p) and the reciprocal of HSA concentration (c) could be described with the Langmuir equation:[48]
(8)
1
Δ
I
p
=
1
Δ
I
pmax
+
1
Δ
I
pmax
K
a
c
Herein, ΔI pmax and K a are the maximum of the current drop and the binding equilibrium constant, respectively. As shown in the insert of Fig. 9A, the binding equilibrium constant K a was calculated as 8.37×105 Lmol−1.

EIS was usually used to investigate the interface property of surface-modified electrode by the value of R ct [49]. The R ct value, is connected with the semicircle diameter at higher frequency in Nyquist plot of EIS, and can be changed with the modified substance on the electrode surface. The increase of R ct value indicates the increase of surface binding and electron transfer blocking. As shown in Fig. 9B, R ct values increased with the increasing concentration of HSA, which indicated that the interaction between HSA and GQDs resulted in the increment of resistance. Furthermore, these results also suggested that HSA could bind with GQDs to form the complex. In order to verify the data obtained by steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, the affinity constant K a from the data of EIS could be calculated from the Langmuir isotherm model:[49]
(9)
R
ct
=
(
R
ct
)
max ⁡
× c ×
K
a
1 +
K
a
c

Herein, c is the concentration of HSA. According to Eq. (9), the affinity constant K a could be calculated from the plot of c/R ct as a function of c. As shown in the insert illustrated in Fig. 9B, the affinity constant K a was calculated to be 1.01×106 Lmol−1.

Since, the actual molecular weight of GQDs was much higher than 78gmol−1, the associative binding constant obtained by spectroscopic methods was lower than the actual binding constant. Hence, the binding constants calculated by electrochemical approaches were higher than the binding constants obtained from spectroscopic methods. Moreover, all possible binding might increase the R ct value in EIS experiments, while the binding occurring mainly around Trp residue was revealed in the fluorescence titration [50]. Therefore, the affinity constant measured from EIS was much higher than that measured from fluorescence spectra.

In this work, we systematically investigated the molecular interaction between GQDs and HSA by multispectroscopic and electrochemical approaches. First, the fluorescence spectroscopy results indicated that GQDs could static quench the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA. The binding interactions existed between GQDs and HSA by the formation of complexes that were stabilized mainly by van der Waal interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions. The binding site of GQDs was mainly site I of HSA and some common ions could partly increase the binding ability of GQDs. Second, three-dimensional spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy and CD spectroscopy proved that some microenvironmental and conformational changes in HSA were induced by their binding interactions, indicating the potential toxicity of GQDs to cause the damage of secondary structure and the disturbance of normal biological functions of HSA. Third, the electrochemical approaches studies reconfirmed the binding interactions between GQDs and HSA that resulted in the complex formation. Overall, these studies provide influential insight into the molecular interaction between GQDs and proteins, which may prove useful in evaluating the toxicity and the biological effects of GQDs in biological and biomedical fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21203035, 21403039), the Guangxi Natural Science Foundation(2013GXNSFCA019005, 2013GXNSFBA019029), the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi Provincial Education Department (2013YB138, ZD2014081) and the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (YCSZ2014186).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2014.11.019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
